coredata.dc:title: Large-surface-area activated carbon with high density by electrostatic densification for supercapacitor electrodes
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yuting
Li, Jing
Jiang, Zimu
Shi, Mengjiao
Sheng, Rui
Liu, Zheng
Zhang, Su
Cao, Yali
Wei, Tong
Fan, Zhuangjun
coredata.dc:description: Although activated carbon has been widely used for supercapacitors, the unsatisfactory volumetric capacitance and rate performance caused by the well-developed porous structure severely hinder its application in minimized and high-power devices. Herein, we develop an electrostatic densification method to prepare large surface area (ca. 3359 m2 g−1) and high density activated carbon materials by using the positively charged chitosan and negatively charged graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the precursors. The strong molecular electrostatic attraction makes the GQDs embedded activated carbon an improved compaction density of 0.75 g cm−3 than the activated carbon without GQDs (3245 m2 g−1, 0.67 g cm−3) and commercial activated carbon (1600 m2 g−1, 0.45 g cm−3). The highly crystallized GQDs can also improve the electrochemical kinetics by constructing the entire conductive networks. Therefore, the sample shows high capacitances of 341 F g−1/256 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 and remarkable rate performance with 70% capacitance retention at 100 A g−1 in three-electrode system. Moreover, the outstanding capacitive performance can maintain at the commercial mass loading of 10 mg cm−2, demonstrating great potential for practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Activated carbon
Electrostatic densification
Volumetric capacitance
Rate performance
Supercapacitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of EDAC. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEM images of (a) EDAC-0, (b) EDAC-0.5, and (c) EDAC-1. (d) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (e) compaction densities and porous structure evolution of EDAC-0, EDAC-0.5, and EDAC-1. (f) Comparison of the specific surface area and compaction density of EDAC-0.5 with other reported activated carbons. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, and (c) XPS survey spectra of the as-prepared EDACs. (d) XPS C1s spectrum of EDAC-0.5. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical performance of the EDACs measured by the three-electrode system: (a) CV curves at 20 mV s−1, (b) galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at 1 A g−1, (c) gravimetric and (d) volumetric capacitances at the current densities from 1 to 100 A g−1 of the EDACs. (e) Gravimetric and volumetric capacitances of EDAC-0.5 compared with the reported activated carbon materials. (f) EIS Nyquist plots of the EDACs (the inset shows the charge-transfer resistance (Rct)). (g) CV curves at different scan rates of EDAC-0.5 at 10 mg cm−2. (h) Nyquist plots and (i) gravimetric capacitances at the current densities of from 1 to 80 A g−1 of EDAC-0.5 at 2 and 10 mg cm−2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The diagram of EDAC-0.5//G/Ni(OH)2 asymmetric supercapacitor assembly. (b) CV curves of the positive electrode G/Ni(OH)2 and the negative electrode EDAC-0.5 at 10 mV s−1. (c) Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves and (d) Ragone plots of the EDAC-0.5//G/Ni(OH)2 asymmetric supercapacitor compared with other reported asymmetric supercapacitors. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Large-surface-area activated carbon with high density by electrostatic densification for supercapacitor electrodes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology
College of Material Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although activated carbon has been widely used for supercapacitors, the unsatisfactory volumetric capacitance and rate performance caused by the well-developed porous structure severely hinder its application in minimized and high-power devices. Herein, we develop an electrostatic densification method to prepare large surface area (ca. 3359 m2 g−1) and high density activated carbon materials by using the positively charged chitosan and negatively charged graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the precursors. The strong molecular electrostatic attraction makes the GQDs embedded activated carbon an improved compaction density of 0.75 g cm−3 than the activated carbon without GQDs (3245 m2 g−1, 0.67 g cm−3) and commercial activated carbon (1600 m2 g−1, 0.45 g cm−3). The highly crystallized GQDs can also improve the electrochemical kinetics by constructing the entire conductive networks. Therefore, the sample shows high capacitances of 341 F g−1/256 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 and remarkable rate performance with 70% capacitance retention at 100 A g−1 in three-electrode system. Moreover, the outstanding capacitive performance can maintain at the commercial mass
loading of 10 mg cm−2, demonstrating great potential for practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have attracted widespread attention in fast energy storage due to their high power density and excellent cycle stability [1–5]. Activated carbons are widely used as electrode materials for supercapacitors because of the high surface area, moderate conductivity, and outstanding electrochemical stability [6,7]. Activated carbons mainly show an electrical double-layer capacitance, which is strongly dependent on the accessible surface area as active sites and meso-/macro-pores as ion migration channels [8]. Therefore, great efforts have been made to prepare large-surface-area and hierarchically porous activated carbons [9–11] previously to ensure high gravimetric capacitance and good rate performance [12,13]. For example, activated carbons with extremely high surface area over 3000 m2 g−1 have been prepared by chemical activation of coal tar pitch [14,15], biomass [16–18], and polymers [19,20] as the precursors, showing high gravimetric capacitance over 300 F g−1. With the rapid development of device miniaturization in portable electronics and renewable energy vehicles, the volumetric performance of electrode materials is increasingly important. The well-developed porous structure of activated carbons usually results in a very low material density and unsatisfactory volumetric performance, severely hindering the practical application.

To meet the demands for high volumetric performance electrode materials, many efforts have been made to develop dense carbon materials. For example, various dense carbon materials with remarkable volumetric performance such as carbide-derived carbon [21–23], graphene assemblies [24–26], doped carbons [27–29], and ultra-microporous carbons [30–33] have been prepared. However, dense activated carbon with a large surface area (>2000 m2 g−1) is hard to prepare because activated carbon is usually produced through an outside-to-inside activation process. Some large-sized pores are inevitably formed due to the inhomogeneous etching of the loose carbon precursors, resulting in the low density and unsatisfactory volumetric performance. Mechanical compression is the most used method to improve the density of activated carbon [34–37]. Yang and co-workers [38] reported a graphene oxide-assisted densification strategy to pack activated carbon into a compact graphene network. The obtained carbon material showed a high specific surface area of 2165 m2 g−1, an improved density of 0.76 g cm−3, and good volumetric capacitance of 138 F cm−3 in ionic liquid electrolyte. These effective methods mainly focus on reducing the macropore volume between activated carbon particles, designing dense activated carbon from the precursor aspect is barely reported before.

Herein, we present an electrostatic densification method for the preparation of large-surface-area and high-density activated carbon. The positively charged chitosan and negatively charged graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are assembled to dense precursor in aqueous solution benefiting from the strong intermolecular electrostatic attraction. Through subsequent carbonization and activation, the optimized electrostatic densified activated carbon (EDAC) shows a remarkably high surface area of 3359 m2 g−1 and a high compaction density of 0.75 g cm−3, which are higher than the activated carbon without GQDs (3245 m2 g−1, 0.67 g cm−3). What’s more, the highly crystallized GQDs help improve the electrochemical kinetics by forming entire conductive networks inside the EDAC. Therefore, the EDAC shows extremely high gravimetric/volumetric capacitances (341 F g−1/256 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1) and remarkable rate performance (70% capacitance retention at 100 A g−1). Moreover, it maintains 277 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 182 F g−1 at 80 A g−1 at the commercial electrode mass loading of 10 mg cm−2, demonstrating a great potential for practical application.
The GQDs are prepared by a chemical oxidation method using bituminous coal powder (Heishan, Xinjiang, China) as the precursor according to our previous works [30,39].

In a typical process, 0.4 g GQDs and 0.8 g chitosan were dispersed into 40 mL deionized water and 80 mL diluted acetic acid solution (5 wt%), respectively, by vigorous stirring and sonication. Then, the GQD dispersion was quickly poured into the chitosan solution under stirring. The viscous gel is rapidly formed due to the strong electrostatic interaction. The mixture was stirred for 12 h and dried at 100 °C. After milling to fine powder, the sample was pre-carbonized at 500 °С for 1 h in N2atmosphere with a heating rate of 2 °С min−1. The samples were named as pre-EDAC-x, in where the “x” stands for the GQDs to chitosan mass ratio. To prepare EDACs, the pre-carbonized products and KOH with a mass ratio of 1: 4 were mixed by milling and then activated at 800 °С for 1 h in N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 5 °С min−1. The obtained powder was immersed in 1 mol L−1 HCl solution to remove the inorganic salts and then washed to neutral using deionized water. The samples with different GQD addition ratios are named as EDAC-x (“x” is the same as for pre-EDACs-x).

Scanning electronics microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL-2100F) were used to investigate the morphology and structure of the samples. The structural analysis was carried out on an X-ray diffraction system (XRD, Bruker D8 Advances) and Raman spectroscopy (Bruker SENTERRA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was texted by an ESCALAB 250 Xi spectrometer. The specific surface areas and pore distribution were characterized by N2 adsorption-desorption at 77 K using an adsorption apparatus (Quantachrome, NOVA 2000), then calculated based on the Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) method and the density functional theory (DFT) model. The details are given in our previous works [7].

The electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 660C electrochemical workstation in 6 mol L−1 KOH aqueous electrolyte. The working electrode was made up of the active material, carbon black, and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) binder at a mass ratio of 85:10:5. They were stirred in ethanol from a slurry and pressed onto the nickel foam (1 cm2) as current collector. The platinum sheet and Hg/HgO were selected as the counter and reference electrodes in the three-electrode system, respectively. The mass loading of the working electrodes were about 2 mg cm−2. EDAC-0.5 was also tested at the mass loading of 10 mg cm−2. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge were performed in the potential window of −0.9–0.1 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured in a frequency ranging from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. Gravimetric capacitance (Cg, F g−1) and volumetric capacitance (Cv, F cm−3) of the electrode materials were calculated from the charge-discharge curves according to the equations:
(1)
C g
= I Δ t / m Δ V
where I is the discharge current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), m is the mass of active material (g), ΔV is the potential window (V).

The volumetric capacitance (Cv) was evaluated according to the following equations:
(2)
C v
=
C g
× ρ
(3)
ρ = m / V
where C
g (F g−1) is gravimetric capacitance, ρ (g cm−3) is the compaction density of active material, m (g) and V (cm3) are certain mass and the volume of active material. We calculated the volume of active material by measuring the radius (r) and thickness (h) of the compressed active material under the pressure of 10 MPa in a cylinder mold (V = h∗π∗r2).

The asymmetric supercapacitors were assembled using graphene loaded Ni(OH)2 composite (G/Ni(OH)2, the preparation method is given in the Supporting Information) and EDAC-0.5 as the positive and negative electrode materials, respectively. The mass loading of the G/Ni(OH)2 and EDAC-0.5 were 2 and 4 mg cm−2 based on the charge balance theory (Equation (4)). The CV and galvanostatic charge-discharge were measured with the potential window of 0–1.65 V.
(4)
m +
/
m −
=
C −
× Δ
V −
/
C +
× Δ
V +

The energy density (E) and power density (P) were calculated by the following equations:
(5)
E =
C
t o t a l
( Δ V )
2
/ 2
(6)
P = E / Δ t
where C
total is the gravimetric capacitance of the asymmetric supercapacitor calculated based on the total mass of the positive and negative electrodes, ΔV is the potential window (V), and Δt is the discharge time (s).

As shown in Fig. 1 , chitosan is an alkaline polysaccharide containing a large number of –NH2. When dissolved in diluted acetic acid, –NH2 combines with H+ to form a positively charged –NH3 + group. GQDs contain various types of oxygen functional groups such as –COOH, CO, and –OH, as shown in the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, XPS spectrum, and thermogravimetric analysis in our previous works [7,30,39]. The ionization of –COOH to –COO- makes the negative charge of GQDs in water. The Zeta potential of GQDs and chitosan were measured as −36.8 and +34.5 mV, respectively (Fig. S1). When the two solutions are mixed, the strong electrostatic attraction between chitosan and GQDs leads to the densification of carbon precursors at a molecular level.

As shown in Fig. 2 a–c, all of the obtained EDACs show a block appearance. EDAC-0 without using GQDs shows a loose structure in which abundant meso- and macro-pores are observed on the rough surface (Fig. 2a and Fig. S2a). By adding an appropriate amount of GQDs, EDAC-0.5 through electrostatic densification shows a dense structure with only a small amount of large pores (Fig. 2b and Fig. S2c). Further, increasing the GQD dosage causes the formation of abundant meso- and macro-pores in EDAC-1 (Fig. 2c) and the reduced compaction density (Fig. 2e). This is because the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups on GQDs during high-temperature treatment results in the rapid gas release and formation of well-developed large-sized pores [39,40], as will be discussed later.

The specific surface area and porous structure of the EDACs were measured by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. All the EDACs show a type I isotherm with rapid adsorption increase at the low relative pressure region (P/P0 < 0.10) and a well-defined plateau at the high relative pressure region (0.20 < P/P0 < 0.99) (Fig. 2d and Fig. S3), meaning the primarily microporous structure and (Fig. S4). EDAC-0.5 shows a remarkably high surface area of 3359 m2 g−1 and total micro- and meso-pores volume of 1.72 cm3 g−1, which are even slightly higher than those of EDAC-0 (3245 m2 g−1 and 1.64 cm3 g−1) (Fig. 2e and Table S1). The slightly increased surface area and pore volume are mainly due to the formation of micropores by decomposing the oxygen-enriched GQDs [30]. Generally, large pore volume is contradicted to high density. However, the electrostatic densification effectively eliminates the macropores structure of the carbon precursors, leading to EDAC-0.5 with a higher compaction density of 0.75 g cm−3 than 0.67 g cm−3 of EDAC-0, as confirmed from the SEM images (Fig. 2a and b). It is also interesting that with the increase of GQD dosage, the specific surface area and total micro- and meso-pore volumes of EDAC-1 are decreased to 2345 m2 g−1 and 1.23 cm3 g−1. The reason is also due to the decomposition of excessive GQDs during pre-carbonization. That is, severe gas release causes the carbon precursor’s loose structure with abundant meso- and macro-pores. In the subsequent activation process, over-activation takes place because of the large contact area of the carbon precursor and KOH, resulting in the coalesced large-sized pores as well as the decreased specific surface area and compaction density [41,42].

To have a deep insight of the pore structure evolution, we have characterized the pre-carbonized samples in detail. As reported in our previous work [7,30,39], the thermogravimetric curve of GQDs in N2 atmosphere shows that the mass residual after 900 °C is ca. 42% because of the gradual decomposition and gas release. From the SEM images (Fig. S5), the surface of pre-EDAC-0 (without adding GQDs) is obviously rough with many meso-/macro-pores. The pre-EDAC-0.5 shows a smooth surface and a dense structure with the introduction of appropriate amount of GQDs, demonstrating the effect of GQDs for electrostatic densification. With the increase of GQD dosage, the surface of pre-EDAC-1 becomes rough again. From the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (Fig. S6), the specific surface area and meso-pores volume gradually increase from 3.46 to 9.81 m2 g−1 and 0.003–0.016 cm3 g−1 with the increase of GQD dosage (Table S2). In addition, from the mercury intrusion analysis, the macro-pore volume first decrease from 0.85 cm3 g−1 of pre-EDAC-0 to 0.76 cm3 g−1 of pre-EDAC-0.5, then increase to 0.81 cm3 g−1 of pre-EDAC-1 (Table S3). Meanwhile, the bulk densities increase from 0.57 to 0.66 g cm−3, then slightly decrease to 0.61 g cm−3. The results are in well accordance with the above (Fig. S5). Proper GQD dosage not only allows strong electrostatic densification, but also prevents the severe gas release. Therefore, pre-EDAC-0.5, as well as its derivation EDAC-0.5, shows a relatively low meso-/macro-pore volume and a high density. It is also proved that decomposition of excessive GQDs causes the formation of abundant meso-/macro-pores in pre-EDAC-1, also resulting in the decreased compaction density and surface area of EDAC-1. As a result, EDAC-0.5 shows the integration of large surface area and high compaction density, which are superior to the commercial activated carbon YP-50 (1600 m2 g−1, 0.45 g cm−3) and most of the reported large-surface-area activated carbons (Fig. 2f) [16,38,43–46]. The higher compaction density of EDAC-0.5 (0.75 g cm−3) than mercury intrusion density of pre-EDAC-0.5 (0.66 g cm−3) may due to several reasons: 1) some loose particles are removed by severe chemical etching during chemical activation due to their high reactivity, and 2) strong mechanical compression (also used for preparing the electrodes) leads to a more compact packing of the carbon particles.

Our previous work shows that embedding highly crystallized GQDs helps to construct entire conductive networks inside the activated carbon [7]. From the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Fig. S2d), many highly crystallized domains are observed inside EDAC-0.5, leading to a more than 2 times higher electric conductivity of EDAC-0.5 (187.25 S m−1) compared with 87.15 S m−1 of EDAC-0 (Fig. S7), which is much favorable for improving the capacitive and rate performance for supercapacitors.

The XRD patterns show two diffraction peaks at around 21 and 43°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) plane of carbon (Fig. 3 a) [7]. The dispersive peaks indicate the amorphous feature of EDACs with a well-developed porous structure. The tilted curves at a small angle below 15° indicate the enriched microporous structure. With the increase of GQD dosage, the (002) peak of EDAC-1 slightly shifts from 23° to 20° due to the lattice distortion caused by the randomly packed GQDs [30,39]. The Raman spectra of EDACs can be fitted as four Gaussian peaks centering at 1210, 1360, 1510, and 1600 cm−1 (Fig. 3b), corresponding to the T, D, D′, and G-band, respectively [47]. The ratio of D and G peaks (AD/AG) represents the defect density of carbon materials. EDAC-0.5 shows a higher surface area and larger pore volume than EDAC-0 but a similar AD/AG ratio, revealing the formation of entire conductive networks by the highly crystallized GQDs [7]. As further confirmed by the obviously decreased AD/AG ratio of EDAC-1 to 1.68 in the Raman spectrum, excessive GQDs and partially removed amorphous carbon derived from chitosan through over-activation lead to the greatly reduced defect density. The embedded conductive works in EDACs can significantly improve the surface area utilization and rate performance for supercapacitors.

The XPS spectra of EDACs show two peaks at around 284.3 and 532.4 eV, corresponding to the C1s and O1s components, respectively (Fig. 3c). There is also a small amount of nitrogen coming from the pyrolysis of chitosan in the EDACs (Table S4). The electrostatic densified carbon precursor shows a decreased contact surface area toward KOH, thus an improved chemical inertness. Therefore, the dense EDAC-0.5 shows the highest C/O atom ratio of 9.99 than that of the other two samples with abundant meso- and macro-porous structure. The XPS C1s spectra of EDAC-0.5 and other samples are deconvoluted into three peaks located at 284.5, 285.8, and 287.9 eV, corresponding to the CC, C–O, and CO components, respectively (Fig. 3d and Fig. S8) [48]. The XPS O1s and N1s spectra of EDACs are presented in Fig. S9. The N1s spectra shows three peaks located at 397.8, 400.2, and 402.3 eV, corresponding to the pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N components, respectively. The contents of oxygen and nitrogen components are shown in Tables S5 and S6. These nitrogen- and oxygen-bearing functional groups can provide additional pseudo-capacitance [49].

The electrochemical performance of EDACs was first investigated by a three-electrode system. The CV curves at 20 mV s−1 (Fig. 4 a) and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 4b) deviate from the rectangle and isosceles triangle shapes, indicating the combination of electrical double layer capacitance from the large surface area and pseudo-capacitance from the nitrogen- and oxygen-bearing functional groups. EDAC-0.5 shows the highest gravimetric capacitance of 341 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a remarkable rate performance of 238 F g−1at 100 A g−1 (70% capacitance retention), which are the highest among the samples (Fig. 4c). The capacitance contributions were calculated based on the Trasatti method (Fig. S10) [47,50–53]. The electrical double layer capacitance contribution of EDAC-0.5 is 77% at the scan rate of 2 mV s−1, and rapidly increase to 91% at 20 mV s−1, demonstrating that the electrical double layer capacitance from large surface area of EDAC-0.5 is the dominated energy storage behavior at high rate due to the robust electrochemical kinetics. Although EDAC-1 shows lower capacitances (273 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and 198 F g−1 at 100 A g−1) than EDAC-0.5, it performs a slightly better rate performance with the capacitance retention of 72% at 100 A g−1, revealing that entire conductive networks constructed by the highly crystallized GQDs play an essential role for improving the electrochemical kinetics. Under the optimized condition, the CV curves and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of EDAC-0.5 also show in Fig. S11. Moreover, owing to the high compaction density (0.75 g cm−3) of EDAC-0.5, it yields the high volumetric capacitance of 256 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 and 179 F cm−3 at 100 A g−1 (Fig. 4d). The gravimetric and volumetric capacitances of EDAC-0.5 are superior to most of the previously reported activated carbons and some other carbon materials (Fig. 4e) [11,16,43,44,54–57].

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to measure the electrochemical kinetics of the materials (Fig. 4f). The diameters of the semicircles in the high frequency region for EDACs are dramatically decreased with the increase of GQD dosage, leading to a gradually decreased charge-transfer resistance from 0.189 Ω of EDAC-0 to 0.114 Ω of EDAC-0.5 and 0.109 Ω of EDAC-1. This further demonstrates that embedding highly crystallized GQDs is much favorable to improve the electrochemical kinetics of the activated carbon. Moreover, EDAC-0.5 shows a nearly vertical line toward the real axis in the low frequency region, indicating the robust ion migration behavior originated from sufficient mesopores as transport channels. Therefore, the outstanding capacitive and rate performance of EDAC-0.5 is owing to the robust electrochemical kinetics coming from highly efficient electron and ion transport paths.

To achieve the demand of high-efficiency and low-cost energy storage, improving the total energy density through increasing the active mass loading on supercapacitor electrode is crucial for practical application [58]. On the other hand, one of the effective way to increase the voltage window and energy density (based on equation E = 0.5CV2) of aqueous supercapacitors is assembling asymmetric supercapacitors using carbon and pseudocapacitive materials as the negative and positive electrode materials, respectively. Since the pseudocapacitive materials usually show more than several times higher capacitance than carbon, to balance the capacitance, the mass loading of carbon electrodes should be higher than that of the positive electrodes. Therefore, maintaining good electrochemical performance of carbon materials at high mass loadings is of great importance for practical supercapacitors, but still challenging due to the continually blocked electron and ion transport paths. To investigate the potential for practical application, we measured the electrochemical performance of EDAC-0.5 at a commercial mass loading of 10 mg cm−2 in a three-electrode system. The CV curves of the EDAC-0.5 have no obvious shape change at different scan rates (Fig. 4g), indicating that the electrode still maintains the robust electrochemical kinetics, as demonstrated by the overlapped Nyquist plots of the electrodes at 2 and 10 mg cm−2 (Fig. 4h). As expected, EDAC-0.5 at 10 mg cm−2 still shows 277 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and outstanding rate performance of 182 F g−1 at 80 A g−1 (Fig. 4i).

In order to further evaluate the application performance, we assembled asymmetric supercapacitor by using the as-prepared G/Ni(OH)2 as the positive electrode and the EDAC-0.5 as the negative electrode (Fig. 5 a). The electrochemical performance of the as-prepared G/Ni(OH)2 was investigated in a three-electrode system (Fig. S12). A pair of redox peaks in the 0–0.5 V vs. Hg/HgO indicates the pseudocapacitive behavior of G/Ni(OH)2 (Fig. 5b and Fig. S5a). G/Ni(OH)2 shows a high specific capacitance of 1557 F g−1 at 2 mV s−1 and 778 F g−1 when the scan rate is increased to 50 mV s−1 (Fig. S13d). The EDAC-0.5 to G/Ni(OH)2 mass ratio calculated from the CV curves is 2:1. Fig. 5c shows the galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of EDAC-0.5//G/Ni(OH)2 asymmetric supercapacitor at various current densities. The non-triangular curves indicate the contribution of the pseudo-capacitance from G/Ni(OH)2. It is worth mentioning that the energy densities of EDAC-0.5//G/Ni(OH)2 asymmetric supercapacitor are 79 Wh kg−1 at 337 W kg−1 and 31 Wh kg−1 at a high power density of 6508 W kg−1, which is comparable with or higher than those of the previously reported asymmetric supercapacitors in alkaline electrolytes (Fig. 5d) [59–64], showing great potential for practical application.

In summary, we have developed an electrostatic densification method for the preparation of large surface area and dense activated carbon by using the positively charged chitosan and negatively charged GQDs as the precursors. The obtained activated carbon material exhibits high specific surface area of 3359 m2 g−1 and compaction density of 0.75 g cm−3, which are better than most of the reported activated carbons. Moreover, the highly crystallized GQDs contribute to robust electrochemical kinetics through formation of the entire conductive networks. Under the optimized condition, the sample shows high capacitances of 341 F g−1/256 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 and remarkable rate performance with 70% capacitance retention at 100 A g−1 in three-electrode system. At a high mass loading of 10 mg cm−2, it still maintains a high capacitance of 277 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and outstanding rate performance. As a proof of concept, the EDAC-0.5//G/Ni(OH)2 asymmetric supercapacitor delivers high energy density and power density. This work provides a new thought for the preparation of dense carbon materials from the precursor aspect, which may also apply for other high volumetric energy storage devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (2020D01C019), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51702275, 52062046, 51972342, and 51872056), Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province (ts20190922), Key Basic Research Projects of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong province (ZR2019ZD51), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019TQ0353).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.01.016.
